CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The resolution of the majority of the Dublin Coipoe
Ceanncalp  at a ‘recent meeting concerning the
TrapErR incident was bad enough in all  con-

 science; but the Dublin Coipoe Cesnncain is only
‘a local body ; the resolution passed at the last meeting
e Executive Committee of the Gaelic League was
itely worse. We had hoped that this week we
have been able to bury the incident; unfor-
tunately, the Executive Committee have only aggra-
the situation. The chief thing about the resolu-
 passed at the meeting las Thursday is the fact

t it studiously abstained from the expression of the

1 ot to: Dr. McWalter, T.C., and his friends for

oy behaviour of the roughs at the procession.

Now either the majority at the meeting felt no regret, in
which case they would be actual sympathisers with the
roughs, or though feeling regret, they were afraid to
express it; perhaps the majority was composed of
those who felt no regret and those who felt regret but
were afraid to express it—in any case the gravity of the
situation is patent to all.

The Language movement is above all parties, persons
and cliques; the language is the fulerum upon which
only an Irish and distinct civilisation can be lifted into
cxistence; but the policy and conduct of a special
organisation like the Gaelic League that has to do con-
siderably with the desperate attempt to rehabilitate the
language in face of stupendous difficulties is a matter
that, of course, no one concerned in the greater question
of a distinct Irish civilisation can afford to overlook.

For some time past, it is plain,. there has been a lack
of tact, indeed an abundance of what we might call
anti-tact displayed by some of the powers that be in
the organisation. With a month to consider the matter
and in face of the various resolutions, protests and so
forth, both public and private, we were confident that
the Executive Committee would be wise, firm and
strong enough to do the tactful thing and so put an
end to the ugly crisis precipitated by street rowdies.
The Executive Committee has shown about as much
tact as the rest, with the result that the situation is
now worse than ever. Let us recall that attacks on the
LEADER were made at a meeting before Language Week
over which one of the Vice-Presidents of the League
presided ; a paper that has been called a “ confrere
by the official organ of the League suggested before
Language Week that every shilling given to the Lan-
guage Fund would be a blow at the” LEADER, and sub-
sequent to that the official, officials or Committee con-
cerned ostentatiously advertised the collection in the
“ confrere ”’ of the official organ that had suggested that
every shilling given to the Fund would be a blow at
the LEADER and as ostentatiously boycotted the latter
paper. After the street rowdyism at the Procession,
this. particular “ confrere ’’ of the official organ of the
Gaelic League clapped the street rowdies on the back
and approved their conduct. Is the majority of the
Executive Committee in sympathy with the roughs and
their friends? Or, if not in sympathy with them, are
they afraid to offend them by expressing regret to Dr.
McWalter, T.C., to Mr. Gore, and to the Editor of
this paper? That is the natural question, the inevitable
question, which the resolution passed at last Thursday’s
meeting must suggest to every intelligent man who
follows the matter. If the Executive Committee of the
Gaelic League can be dictated to by the friends of street
brawlers, then the outlook for the organisation as at
present constituted is ominous indeed. Now, at the
Dublin Coipoe Ceanncaip ab which ten men attended,
the following resolution was passed : —*“ While we regret
that even in one isolated instance the harmony of the
Language Procession should have been disturbed, yet,
as the incident referred to was caused by persons acting
without authority, and was due to the unauthorised
introduction of an individual into the section reserved
for the Corporation, we do not consider that action on
our part is'called for.”” That resolution only aggravated
the situation, and since then we have been receiving
protests, and several branches of the Gaelic League have
passed resolutions. At Thursday’s meeting of the
Executive Committee, the following resolution was
passed :— While we regret that even in one isolated
instance the harmony of the language procession should
haye been disturbed, yet as the incident referred to

was cau_sed by persons acting without authority, we do 
not consj.der that action on our part is called for and 
we call ·on all true friends of the movement to ~void 
further controversy in the matter." 
It will be noted 
that this r~solution is the same as the absurd and 
loudly condemned resolution of the Dublin 
Co1r'Oe 
Ce"nm:;A11, but for the fact that the words "and was 
due. to the unauthorised introduction of an individual 
into a section reserved for the Corporation " are 
omitted, and that the exhortation " and we 'call on 
all t.rue friends of the movement to n.void further con• 
troversy in the matter" are added. 
VVe, for our part, 
beg to suggest that " all true friends of the movement ' ' 
would be better employed in disa:ssociating a noble and 
honourable cause from all connection with street blaok-
guardism and foot-pad tactics. 
It is for the 1mr• 
ticular Irish public concerned to differentiate if they 
can .between tweedledum and tweedledee. 
There is no 
apology to Dr. McWalter, T.C., to his friend Mr. John 
Gore, or to the Editor of this paper. What are these 
trio compa,red to the street roughs, their aiders, ab-
betors and applauders 1 Were there men who must not 
be disobeyed by the majority of the Coir-oe 511ot" at 
this meeting around the corner on Thursday night last ? 
The resolution of the Executive Committee appears 
to us to be quite absurd as well as an aggravation of 
the insult offered to Dr. M'Walter, T.C., and his friends 
in the streets of Dublin on the occasion of the Proces-
sion. 
Surely it was unnecessary to formally declai:e 
that that body regretted that the harmony of the Dub-
hn Procession was disturbed; we should have thought 
that that might be taken for granted; all people con-
cerned in any enterprise natw-ally wish that it should 
be carried out harmoniously, and one would think it 
rather unnecessary for them to state that, street roughs 
intervening, they regretted the want of harmony. 
Of 
cow-se they did; so would any collection of human be-
ings; and the particular want of harmony having led 
to other more serious disturbances in the way of branch 
resolutions, protests, etc., naturally the regret is inten-
sified. 
Surely no one who protested and asked for an 
apology thought that the Co1r'Ce 5110.t,1 organised the 
street-rough escapade! 
Why, then, point out that the 
blackguardism was enacted by " persons acting without 
authority " 
The Organisation, in many devious ways, 
as we have over and over again shown, had got itself 
expressly mixed up in the matter, yet the Executive 
Committee did not feel called upon to express even a 
formal regret 'to D1:. M'Walter, T.C., and his friends on 
account of the blackguardism to which they were sub· 
jected by roughs at the Procession, one of whom wore a 
badge upon his arm . The absurd resolution as it stands 
really in a round-about way emphatically says :- " We 
don't wish to, or we dare not, offer any apology, 
or denounce the conduct of 
the street roughs.'' 
That is the meaning of the resolution in so far as it 
means anything; and it appears to ·us that the long-
drawn-out exhibition of lack of tact is fully capped by 
this resolution of the Executive Committee who have 
added aggravation after the Dublin Co1r-oe Ce.<11111-
C.(\IJI had added insult to injury. 
Supposing that a vofo of condolence was proposed at a 
meeting of responsible persons to the relatives of some-
one who had recently died, and supposing that meeting 
resolved that as they had nothing to do with bringing 
about the death of the person they considered that no 
action on their part was called for-what would be 
thought of such a resolution 1 Dr. M'W•alter and his 
friends are to receive no apology as the Executive Com-
mittee did not authorise the roughs--one with a badge 
on his arm-to hold up the carriage of a member of the 
Dublin Corporation invited to the Procession ! Those 
branches of the Gaelic League and individuals who have 
expressed their regret at the blackguardism must . be 
i:adly lacking in that luminous and subtle intellectua-
lity which, perhaps, for ought we know, may distin-
guish the resolution of the Executi:ve Committee; for 
these did not authorise the blackguarclism either, al-
though they vigorously condemned it I 
We note that 
the ExecuUve, having emphaticii.lly declined ~o e~press 
any regret to D1·. M'Walter, T.0., and his friends·, 
and having declined to denounce the street roughs, 
trust that no more will be said about the matter. That 
also is brilliant.. 
If a company owed five thousand 
pounds to their creditors, and resolved that t~ey would 
not pay it, it would be very brilliant on their part to 
suggest that, in the interests of peace and quietness, 
there should be no more said about the matter by the 
creditors and their friends. 
The Executive Committee 
surely owed an expression of regret to Dr. M'Wal~er, 
T.C., and his guests. Either not wishing or not danng 
to pay it, they hope that no more will be said about 
t:heir liabilit,y. 
Now, supposing that Dr. M'Walter and his friends 
did not keep control of their tempers, and suppose that 
one of the roughs applauded by a " confrere " of the 
official organ of the Gaelic League was struck, and tliat 
a riot and bloodshed ensued, as easily might have hap-
pened; supposing a few corpses were laid out in the 
Morgue, would the Executive Committee resolve that as 
they did not authorise the slaughter that no action on 
their part was called for ? Who will be the victim next 
yead 
Supposing that the .Ai·chbishop of Dublin at-
tended the Procession next year, and that be was as-
sa,ulted by a gang of roughs, one with a badge on his 
sleeve, would the E.xecutive Committee, in solemn con-
clave assembled, say that as the roughs assaulted the 
Archbishop without authority, no action was called for 
on their part? 
Is the influence represented by street roughs dominant 
in the Central Counsels of the Gaelic League 7 
Is the 
policy of the Executive dictated by fear of street roug4s 
and their friends 7 We had hoped to be able to bury 
the incident in this week's issue ; the Executive Com-
mittee have not seen well to dig a pit in which we could 
place it and cover it up. 
They have aggravated a dis-
agreeable situation, and have refu§ed to rub the gutter 
off the organisation with which the roughs at the Lan-
guage Procession succeeded in fouling it. 
We feel sure that the members of England's Faithful 
Garrison in Ireland, the reo111i11i, the West Britons and 
other enemies of Ireland feel that they owe a debt of 
gratitude to the DublinC01rueCe,,1111c<1111, to the roughs, 
and last but not least to the mem hers of the Executive 
Committee responsible for the absurd resolution passed 
last Thursday. 
The resolution of the majority at the Executive Com-
mittee meeting is in opposition to several of 
the 
branches who had thought it well to declare upon 
the matter beforehand. 
We do not know how many 
branches thought well to move in the matter; we pre-
sume that most branches, looking forward confidently to 
the Executive Committee doing the tactful and the right 
thing, took no action in the hope that the crisis would 
be got over by proper action on the part of the Execu-
tive. 
The resolution of the Executive is in op-
position to the Gavan Branch, the committee of which 
unanimously agreed in asking whether any step had 
been or would be taken to repudiate the action of the 
roughs. 
It is opposed to the view of the Father 
Sherlock Branch, Birmingham, whose President wrote 
in a letter already published by us that he was authorised 
by tha~ branch " to_ protest in its name against the in-

It is out of touch with the Gaelic League in Limerick

the rowdies ; for we understand it was felt that it would
be better not to do anything that might resemble a vote
of censure on the Executive until the Executive had had
an opportunity of doing something itself. The Execu-
tive has had its opportunity, and has refused to con-
demn the rowdies or express regret to Dr. M‘Walter and
his friends, so that it is opportune that the resolution
passed at the general meeting of the Cookstown (Co.
Tyrone) Branch of the Gaelic League should now be pub-
lished.

the Movement, we are of opinion that the Executive
should, at the earliest opportunity, make ample apology
for this most regrettable incident.”

Miceat O Pogluvas, a strenuous worker in the Irish
Language movement, in Manchester, has sent a letter in
the following terms to the General Secretary of the
Gaelic League, Dublin:—

99 Heslington St., Moss Side, Manchester, April
14th, 1905.—A Chara—Personally I'm surprised and
disgusted with how the Executive have—up to now—
dealt with the LEADER affair, they should have publicly
disclaimed any connection with it instead of trying to
shift the responsibility on to the body who were in
charge of the Procession. The systematic boycotting of
the Lesaper by 4An Claidheamh, and the favouring of
other papers with advertisements which are withheld
from the LEADER is doing the League no good, and the
sooner this is recognised the better for all concerned.
As an instance—last year my sister, brother, and self
collected close on £20, this was in addition to doing our
share of Church-door collecting, etc., and most of the
work in connection with the collection here in Man-
chester. This year we have not collected—outside of
doing our ordinary collecting at Church doors, etc.—
twenty pence, owing to this disgusting exhibition in the
Dublin streets. We are aware that the editor of the
LEADER has since asked Gaelic Leaguers not to let the
incident interfere with their work on behalf of the Gae-
lic League, but instead to work all the harder. T am
sorry, however, that I will not follow the advice he
gives, and next year I shall take no part whatever in
the collection unless the affair is seltled satisfactorily.
The Cuman na nGaedhail apparently were the cause of
the trouble, and I am of opinion they were also, in a
great measure, the cause of the badness of the collection
here. You have instances of how, against your own
telegraphed orders, they sent collectors to Churches out-
side their area where we were also collecting. Such are
the people that get lauded to the skies on every possiblo
occasion, whilst the editor of the Leaner, who has, in
his own way, practically made the Gaelic League the
force it is, gets insulted in the Language Procession in
Dublin, and all because he asked for a statement of ac-
count—a statement which every business Gaelic Leaguer
knows is the individual right of every paying member.
I am a firm believer in the LEADER and its policy, and,
as an individual Gaelic Leaguer wishing to show the
editor that I protest against the insult offered him, T am
sending him a copy of this letter.—Yours faithfully,

miceal O Fosluda.

‘meted out to you by the ‘ Tin Pike’ gang can but serve

to still more impress all well-wishers of Irish Ireland

Mr. P. Egan, Fort View, Kiltimagh, Co. Mayo, in
the course of a letter, writes : —“ May I l;e permitted
to nominate myself as one of your sympathisers in your
recent conflict with the Gaelic Lcague'sﬁorncl’-boys at
the Language Procession. T will not begin to write for-
mally on the matter else I would not stop writing 'for
“ten long days and ten long nights,” and in all that time
T don’t think I could write down one word of condemna-
tion for anything written in the Leaper since T became.
acquainted with it some three years ago. I certainly
would not tell you like that southern correspondent, that.
you should not be wasting the space of the LEADER and
your own time in keeping the incident well before your
eaders week after week till reparation is made in the
shape of those responsible for the'organisatwn of the
Procession giving a public denunciation of the conduct of
the roughs—ninny-headed roughs of that ilk are a great
nuisance and a hindrance to any organisation \v]uclli they
hang on to; and from my way of looking at things I
fancy the Gaelic League are making themselves very
small to wink at the conduct of these uncultivated,
brainless bluffs; and they will certainly lose much of
their prestige if they refuse to condemn the conduct of
those who stopped the carriage in which you were driving
at the League Procession.”’

Dear Sir,—I am one of those who in the Irish Inde-
pendent asked for a clearer condemnation by the Coipoe
Snota of the insult offered to you in the Language
Procession. Since that time many influential corres-
pondents also have in your own columns asked in strong
terms for both repudiation and an apology, and, indeed,
on all sides the hope was entertained and freely ex-
pressed that at its first meeting after the “incident ™
the Central Exccutive would do the right thing. The
report, however, as published in this morning’s papers
of the meeting held last Thursday, entirely shatters any
hope that might have been entertained on the point.
For not even a sentiment of regret for the insult to you,
much less an apology, is tendered. No condemnation,
no assurance that such conduct shall not be repeated.

“ Three Railways and a Bank ’ was not noticed, as

land. We note in the current number of Mr. Stead’s
Review of Reviews that one of the paragraphs in “ The
Progress of the World ”’ department of that periodical,
is side-headed * Protestant Ascendancy in Ireland.”
We quote the entire paragraph:—*Whenever Irish
affairs come up for discussion, an attempt is made to
excite the anti-Papistical prejudice by assertions that
whenever Irish Catholics control any public body, it
is a case of ‘ No Protestants need apply.” In reality, as
everyone knows, the boot is on the other leg. In that
Catholic country the well-paid appointments have for
generations been regarded as Protestant perquisites.

" The rule, ‘No Catholics need apply,” appears to have

extended far beyond the Government Offices.  The
Leaper, of Dublin, has published a reprint, entitled
‘ Three Railways and a Bank,” in which a return is
given of the number of Catholics and Protestants em-
ployed by three leading Irish Railway Companies, and
by the Provincial Bank of Ireland. The result is some-
what confounding to those who are always asserting
that the Union secures equal rights for men of all
religions. It may; but somehow or other the Protes-
tants seem to pick up all the fat berths.”” Perhaps the
many papers that do not see their way to quote the
LeapERr, of Dublin,”” may quote this paragraph from
the Review of Revieus, of London.

‘We have received a copy of the first number of a new
monthly periodical entitled 7he Irish Manufacturers’
and Traders’ Journal which is brought out by Mr. Kevin
J. Kenny. The price of this new monthly journalistic
venture is 3d. per single copy, or 2s. 6d. per annum,

We learn with pleasure that Mr. F. J. Roe, better
known to workers in the Irish-Ireland Cause in Dublin
as Mac Fiolla Ruard has become one of the repre-
sentatives of the firm of P. O'Reilly, Poolbeg Street, the
well-known box-makers, ete. It is fitting that a man
like Mr. Roe, who has worked so hard and earnestly in
the Irish-Ireland Movement, should have his business
interests running parallel with the progress of Irish in-
dustry. We congratulate Mr. Roe, and we are sure
that our Dublin readers, who may come in contact with
him in his business capacity, will be all the better
pleased to do business with him when they know that
he has been for years an earnest worker in the Lan-
guage Movement.

Intending—or rather nominally intending—competi-
tors at dancing competitions at various feissanna have
frequently been a source of unnecessary trouble to those
responsible for the working of a feis. It was, to some
extent, a custom for many. dancers to send on their
names to the Feis Secretary without having fully made
up their minds to compete. This practice naturally led
to trouble, and to extra expense in making out the Fe:s
programmes. This year the Committee of the Munster
Feis have decided that each intending competitor in a
dancing competition shall pay a small entrance fee. In
the circumstances we think that the arrangement is a
very good one; and it will probably, where the need
exists for it, be generally adopted.

Ireland, as is well known, has for generations been a
dairy country; but it is a strange fact that up to very
lately not one single item of the machinery required for
the creamery industry of this country was manufactured
in Ireland. Indeed, at the present day only a very
limited quantity of dairy machinery is built within our
shores. There is no reason whatever why this should be
50, and we are glad to note that the effort which the
Dairy Supply Co., Ltd., of King Street, Cork, and Chi-
chester Street, Belfast, have made in this respect has
met with a fair share of encouragement, and since the
company started the manufacture of dairy machines in
this country, we are assured that they have had no rea-
son to regret their step, as they have turned out quite a
large number of creamery steam engines, milk pumps,
cream and milk coolers, along with such machinery as
shafting, power transmission plant, ete. The company,
at their works in Belfast, design, build and manufacture
throughout, their special ‘‘ Shamrock *’ creamery steam
engines, of sizes varying from 6 to 50 brake horse power,
and they also turn out a large quantity of their effective
patent milk pumps, besides the fact that they have, at
least, manufactured in their Irish works something like
100 coolers for milk, cream, etc., up to the present. It
is pleasant to hear that this manufacturing company ex-
port large quantities of goods manufactured by them in
Treland ‘to England and Scotland. We hear that the
company keop about 20 to 30 men employed all the year
round in the manufacture of dairy machinery.

THE “LEADER’S” INDUSTRIAL POLICY AND
THE MANUFACTURERS,

On Saturday last we sent letters—a copy of which
we set out below—to a large number of Irish manu-
facturers : —

Dear Sirs,—The Lraper from the start sought to
arouse the Irish consumer to purchase, and give pre-
ference to, Irish manufactured goods; and from the
start it laid down the general condition (odd particular
cases are open to particular consideration) that Irish
goods on the market should be as good and as cheap as
their outside competitors. Many were sceptical regard-
ing the permanence of the industrial movement, having
little faith in mere sentimental props to industries; but
by placing the movement on the firm rock of common
sense, business principles, and unassailable argument,
it strongly appealed to the good sense of the public, and
the movement has not only lasted but has gone on
gaining strength year by year. Legislative Tariff pro-
tection for Irish goods being only an academic question
—TIreland having no power to put up legally binding
tariffs—that matter is outside the region of practical
discussion,

Many Irish Industries have lived in face of the
general apathy and prejudice against them. At the
present day, notwithstanding all the propaganda work
that has been carried on in favour of supporting Irish
goods, Irish industries have to fight against abhezwy
vglumc of adverse fixed opinion and habit. It is our
view that if Irish-made goods got a mere preferential
support on their merits alone, that the manufacturers
of Ireland would have far more orders than they could
cope with until they had considerably expanded their
works, and that any expansion of existing industries
brought about on these lines would be a sound business
adv.rmce—zu{l advance that could be maintained on strict
business principles backed up by the common sense of
the Trish consumers. If it were possible—which we do
not believe—to create any Ivish industry on the basis of
the public voluntarily giving more than competition
terms, such an industry, depending upon an: unstable
foundation, would necessarily be unstable and would
meet with disaster in a short time.

Messrs. M. Mahony & Bros., Ltd., Blarney, write:—
17th April, 1905.
Dear SiR—We thank you for your letter of 15th inst.
on the subject of Irish Industries, and the degree of sup-
port which Irish manufacturers ought to get from the
public. In my opinion the only true foundation for
success is to give, at least, equal value in every way to
what foreign makers offer. The Irish maker may fairly
ask the Irish public for preference at equal value, but
for nothing more. Any business founded on the hope
of a pecuniary sacrifice on the part of purchasers is
bound to fail.—Yours faithfully,
F. Masony, Director.

Messrs. James Watson and Co., Artists in Stained

trary, we find that the fact of our being in the country
is, in many cases, an incentive to our clients to cut the
prices down.—Yours very truly,
Jas. Warson & Co.

practical development, or those little grants in aid, with-

out which it is impossible to start small industries, or to

foster or make successes of those already in existence that

arc languishing and dying out. In a future issue I

shall show how dismal has been the attempts to revive

certain trades and industries and what a waste of money

has taken place. What a half-hearted way the Depart-

ment has tackled to its work; but, above all, in what

an un-national and unsympathetic way it has so often

met the County Councils, Corporations, and other

Boards and committees who are attempting to put its

schemes into practice. Why is this? It is the old game

of the Castle again—Centralisation—giving no scope to -
the local people, ignoring their suggestions—adopting

the throwing-it-at-its-head policy. Take us as we are,

or not at all. The autocratic ascendancy spirit over

again. We know better than you do. No trust in the

local bodies—we make the rules, you must obey, or no

grant. What can a Scotch lecturer, fresh from the

Lothians, know of Donegal, or a Yorkshireman, of the

county Clare? Why not appoint a native, if he or she

is master of the subject and who have the best qualifi-

cation of all—local knowledge. Local knowledge would
scem, however, to be a disqualifying factor. It’s the
old story over again—Englishmen think Irishmen are
not fit to rule—they think they know much better than
we do; and the trouble of it is Irishmen like Sir
Horace Plunkett cordially endorse that opinion.
